issue_title: RPM build with bdist_rpm failed on RHEL/CentOS-6
issue_body:
On a CentOS-6 host, with these installed (from CentOS or EPEL-6)

```
python-2.6.6-52.el6.x86_64
python-devel-2.6.6-52.el6.x86_64
python-setuptools-0.6.10-3.el6.noarch
zeromq3-3.2.5-1.el6.x86_64
zeromq3-devel-3.2.5-1.el6.x86_64
```

I try to build ```pyzmq``` RPM, which ended in error:

```bash
$ cd pyzmq-14.7.0
$ python ./setup.py bdist_rpm
running bdist_rpm
running egg_info
creating pyzmq.egg-info
...
Executing(%install): /bin/sh -e /var/tmp/rpm-tmp.66FWkg
+ umask 022
+ cd /home/kcwong/build/pyzmq-14.7.0/build/bdist.linux-x86_64/rpm/BUILD
+ cd pyzmq-14.7.0
+ python setup.py install --single-version-externally-managed -O1 --root=/home/kcwong/build/pyzmq-14.7.0/build/bdist.linux-x86_64/rpm/BUILDROOT/pyzmq-14.7.0-1.x86_64 --record=INSTALLED_FILES
usage: setup.py [global_opts] cmd1 [cmd1_opts] [cmd2 [cmd2_opts] ...]
   or: setup.py --help [cmd1 cmd2 ...]
   or: setup.py --help-commands
   or: setup.py cmd --help

error: option --single-version-externally-managed not recognized
error: Bad exit status from /var/tmp/rpm-tmp.66FWkg (%install)


RPM build errors:
    Bad exit status from /var/tmp/rpm-tmp.66FWkg (%install)
error: command 'rpmbuild' failed with exit status 1
```

I think the fix is to change [setup.py](https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/blob/master/setup.py#L29) and unconditionally import ```setuptools```. The reason being the ```bdist_rpm``` command from ```setuptools``` will generate an RPM spec file that calls ```install``` out of ```distutils``` but with that additional option ```--single-version-externally-managed``` that is not understood by ```distutils```. I have tested this fix, and it worked, but I did not do enough regression to verify other setup commands.

issue_title: zmq.SUB hangs on recv()
issue_body:
Hi, 

We are experiencing a reoccurring (1-2 hours interval) "hang" of a python subscriber where it stops receiving data over the socket and thereby "hang" on the recv() call.
The hangs are synchronized between all subscribers regardless when they first subscribed to the socket. 
However if connecting a new subscriber to the same socket after it got stale,  data is received on the socket for the new subscriber but not for the old subscribers that are already stuck. 
Neither the publisher or the subscriber throws any exceptions or show any other signs of problems (memory leaks etc). 

We verified this by using pollers that poll for data on the socket at the subscriber side. 
If  we have not received any data for a timeperiod (default 60 seconds) we recreate the zmq Context. This works every time but at the cost of dataloss. 

The subscriber code with the workaround can be found here: https://github.com/MONROE-PROJECT/Experiments/blob/master/metadata-subscriber/files/metadata_subscriber.py

Old code that hangs forever on recv: https://github.com/MONROE-PROJECT/Experiments/blob/4c2d97f1310c85b38f6c854c3cb9b198112d72c6/metadata-subscriber/files/metadata_subscriber.py

Publisher: 
https://github.com/MONROE-PROJECT/data-exporter/blob/master/metadata_writer_zeromq.c

We have timeframed the error to have started after a upgrade to Debian stretch as the same code for both subscriber and publisher worked in debian jessie. However, we are not sure if this is just a coincidence or due to a change in the underlying libraries.   

The subscriber runs in a docker container and the publisher runs in the host but both run in the same network namespace.
We are using the packaged version of libzmq and pyzmq in Debian stretch:
libzmq-4.2.1
pyzmq-16.0.2
Python-2.7.13 (default, Jan 19 2017, 14:48:08) 
[GCC 6.3.0 20170118] 

Before the error we where using Debian Jessie with these (packaged) library versions:
libzmq-4.0.5
pyzmq-14.4.0
Python-2.7.9 (default, Jun 29 2016, 13:08:31) 
[GCC 4.9.2]

issue_title: --rm-* results in corrupted file
issue_body:
When removing a KMS or PGP master key from a file, the resulting file has an invalid structure and can no longer be read.

```
$ sops --rm-kms arn:aws:kms:eu-central-1:**********:key/cb1fab90-8d17-42a1-a9d8-334968904f94 -r -i allizom.org-20161031.key

$ sops -d allizom.org-20161031.key
Error loading file: Error unmarshalling input json: invalid character 'r' after top-level value
```

Looking through the file, it looks as if the buffer used to write the file back to disk was being reused without being emptied, and string that didn't belong end up breaking the sops metadata.

```yaml
    pgp: []
    unencrypted_suffix: _unencrypted
    version: "1.14"
89e-b86c-57201e06f31e                         <--- this doesn't belong here
    pgp:
    -   fp: 6F73539153B31C193A2154EAF7A9B793541A953D
        created_at: '2016-03-17T00:11:03Z'
```

The same problem could be reproduced with KMS and PGP, and JSON and YAML files.

issue_title: Wrong call in client.js to libdeepspeech
issue_body:
Running the `client.js` against `libdeepspeech` bindings exposes improper values.

```
++ node client.js /tmp/output_graph.pb /tmp/LDC93S1.wav
+ phrase='she had your dark suit in greasy wash water all yeash h h h h h h h h h h h h he e'
```

Looking into the code, `audioBuffer.length` is exactly twice what we have compared to the Python code. The C++ implementation does divide buffer size by two here: https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/af11265dcc6ffd69ce1529ff65ecba3d2da6b473/native_client/client.cc#L163

Difference between C++ code, Python and JS code is that C++ and JS does rely on `sox` or `libsox` while Python does not.

issue_title: [docs] Do not suggest using OIDC Auth backend along with ModelBackend
issue_body:
The installation example in the documentation lists both ModelBackend and OIDC backend

```
# Add 'mozilla_django_oidc' authentication backend
AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS = (
    # ...
    'django.contrib.auth.backends.ModelBackend',
    'mozilla_django_oidc.auth.OIDCAuthenticationBackend',
    # ...
)
```

While this works and believe that's a wrong security paradigm. If you have enabled OIDC you probably don't want ModelBackend enabled as well, as this can leave an open door for attacks outside the org's SSO system.

I suggest that docs urge users to only use the OIDC backend when enabled.

To allow users to only use OIDC please fix  #176 too

issue_title: Declaring traittypes in Containers as uninstantiated classes
issue_body:
Feel free to ignore this, but I wasn't around when #51 was discussed… and this just came up in jupyter/nbconvert#695. So I wanted to weigh in on the question around deprecating a particular way of using uninstantiated traitlets. There are two ways of using traitlets that were deprecated in 4.1 that were not documented in the migration docs http://traitlets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/migration.html. These use cases involve traitlets being used in slightly different ways. I think we should revisit the decision to deprecate one of them. Regardless of the call made in that regard, the change should be included in the migration docs.
Click for more details about these two cases and why I think they are different.

 1. traitlets that are actually instantiated on a class. ```python class MyClass(): foo = Unicode() # ✓ foo = Unicode # deprecated ``` 2. traitlets defining which types are valid inside traitlets subclassed from `Container` (e.g., `List`). ```python class MyClass(): foo = List(Unicode()) # ✓ foo = List(Unicode) # deprecated ``` Deprecating 1. was absolutely the right call. I think that we should revisit the idea of deprecating 2..
Click here for my argument as to why we shouldn't deprecate use case 2.

In case 2, the actual use of a traitlet instance is somewhat strange from a type checking perspective. What you are effectively doing is declaring that the elements of this container need to be of the same _type_ as element that is listed. The only way I've ever seen type checking work involves declaring the _type_s that should be allowed, in python that would be the _class_es that should be allowed. To follow the requirement that we only allow traitlet instances there would be analogous to a type checking syntax that declared that something should be a string by using literal strings. For example, the analogous case from the python type [annotations PEP][pytypes] was: ```python def greeting(name: str) -> str: return 'Hello ' + name ``` but if using our convention it would be ```python def greeting(name: str()) -> str(): return 'Hello ' + name ``` or equivalently: ```python def greeting(name: '') -> '': return 'Hello ' + name ``` I realise that from an implementation point of view this introduces overhead because we also need to be able to put constraints on particular values, which means we can't only work on classes.
**tldr**:; The use case in 2. is similar to type checking, and declaring types as uninstantiated classes is a standard way of specifying types in signatures (including in [python type annotations][pytypes]).
[pytypes]: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/

issue_title: Should travis build fail if source files have wrong whitespace policy?
issue_body:
Now that we have `.editorconfig`, we have an explicit statement of the whitespace policy on the code.

However, nothing is enforcing this on pull requests except manual checking.  This means we can drift back into random whitespace without paying careful attention to whitespace in pull requests by hand.  (Boring and tedious -- isn't this what computers are for?)

Should we enhance the travis build to fail if the source code has whitespace that violates the policy specified in `.editorconfig`?  Mostly, I'm thinking presence of tab characters, wrong newline character and trailing whitespace.  It's probably too annoying to try and write code to check the indent level.

issue_title: Endless migrations creation
issue_body:
Each time when execute `python manage.py makemigrations` it will create new migration:

```
(qb_celery) python manage.py makemigrations
Migrations for 'django_celery_beat':
  /home/user/.virtualenvs/qb_celery/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django_celery_beat/migrations/0015_auto_20171101_1607.py
    - Alter field event on solarschedule
(qb_celery) python manage.py makemigrations
Migrations for 'django_celery_beat':
  /home/user/.virtualenvs/qb_celery/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django_celery_beat/migrations/0016_auto_20171101_1607.py
    - Alter field event on solarschedule
(qb_celery) python manage.py makemigrations
Migrations for 'django_celery_beat':
  /home/user/.virtualenvs/qb_celery/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django_celery_beat/migrations/0017_auto_20171101_1607.py
    - Alter field event on solarschedule
```
The reason for that:

`SOLAR_SCHEDULES = [(x, _(x)) for x in schedules.solar._all_events]`

all_events:
```
_all_events = {
        'dawn_astronomical',
        'dawn_nautical',
        'dawn_civil',
        'sunrise',
        'solar_noon',
        'sunset',
        'dusk_civil',
        'dusk_nautical',
        'dusk_astronomical',
    }
```
so each time, when we import `from celery import schedules` it gives for SOLAR_SCHEDULES dict with different order.

issue_title: Provide caller with information on raised exception after retry
issue_body:
As far as I can tell, when [retryhandler.MaxAttemptsDecorator](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/retryhandler.py#L234) reaches its maximum number of attempts, the retryable exception is returned to the caller. I just spent about 3 hours tracing through boto3 and botocore and writing test code to reproduce a RequestLimitExceeded error, only to find that botocore was properly retrying and the only thing I can think of is that the request limit was really exceeded (even after 5 exponential backoff retries).

It would be very useful in cases like this if the retry handler either (a) wrapped the actual exception in a (e.g.) MaxRetriesExceeded exception, to make it clear that the request was retried and perhaps include the number of retries and maybe the sleep duration, or (b) at least fire an event with this information.

While I do *really* appreciate that the retry logic is so transparent, this also makes it a bit opaque for debugging, as there's really no way I can find to confirm (especially programmatically in the caller) that the request was, in fact, retried and all retries failed.

Thanks,
Jason Antman

(this came up when investigating [this issue](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/issues/187).)

issue_title: KeyError with "context" key in "botocore/awsrequest.py" line 314 create_request_object
issue_body:
When AWS Lambda updated to the newest version of the AWS SDK on the 30th of June, a bug appears to have been introduced, which caused an existing (testing) Lambda function (created from a blueprint) to error out.

https://github.com/boto/botocore/commit/28b8831f5f8518bef5accf994fc377c58a3569f7

```
'context': KeyError Traceback (most recent call last):

File "/var/task/lambda_function.py", line 189, in lambda_handler post_to_es(es_payload) # post to ES with exponential backoff

File "/var/task/lambda_function.py", line 80, in post_to_es es_ret_str = post_data_to_es(payload, es_region, creds, es_endpoint, '/_bulk')

File "/var/task/lambda_function.py", line 47, in post_data_to_es req = botocore.awsrequest.create_request_object(params)

File "/var/runtime/botocore/awsrequest.py", line 314, in create_request_object request_object.context.update(r['context'])

KeyError: 'context'
```

To work around this issue, within my Lambda Function code, I added the context key:value pair as:

```
params = {'method': method, 'url': proto + host + path, 'region': region, 'headers': {'Host': host}, 'body': payload, 'context': {'unknown': 'values'}}
	req = botocore.awsrequest.create_request_object(params)
```

instead of the prior value:

```
params = {'method': method, 'url': proto + host + path, 'region': region, 'headers': {'Host': host}, 'body': payload}
	req = botocore.awsrequest.create_request_object(params)
```

CCN For the product-If you do not have your software classified with an ECCN, please kindly answer the following questions so that we may self-assess:

1, Does the Software perform any encryption or utilize any encryption processes? Y/N
2. If the answer is YES to question 1, please indicate if the encryption is coded into the application or separately called (such as using SSL)
3. If the answer is YES to question 1, please indicate what function(s) the cryptography/encryption serves

A, Copyright protection purposes (Includes using a license key/code)
B, User authentication purposes
C, A core part of the functionality such as to encrypt databases
D, To encrypt communications between the software and a host system.

Background information:
An Export Control Classification Number (ECCN) is a specific alpha-numeric code that identifies the level of export control for items e.g. software that are exported from member states of the Wassenaar Arrangement, including the United States. After obtaining the ECCN, the exporter must determine whether an export license is required to export items to certain countries.

issue_title: git log -L rejected despite being correct
Fetching from remote with explicit refspec raises error incorrectly
issue_body:
GitPython version: 1.0.2
File: git/remote.py
Line: 646
OS: Ubuntu 14.04LTS

Passing a refspec to git fetch through a remote, as in
`repo.remotes.origin.fetch("+refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*")`
will raise an error if the remote has no fetch refspec defined in the config:
```
AssertionError: Remote '%s' has no refspec set.
You can set it as follows: 'git config --add "remote.origin.fetch +refs/heads/*:refs/heads/*"'.
```
Also, note that the error message is incorrectly formatted because the remote's name is replaced only in the second line of the message (a %s appears in the first line of the final message).

The `_assert_refspec()` method should not be called if one or more refspecs were given as arguments to `fetch()`.

issue_title: Close window Problem
issue_body:
If you close a pop-up window using close window, all further Selenium2 keywords fail with an exception:
NoSuchWindowException: Message: Window not found. The browser window may have been closed.
Even keywords as "get window names" fail. 
The same problem arises when the pop-up window is closed by mouse click or using Javascript window.close(). 

Minimal set-up for testing: Simple local html file with 
<button id="bt1" onclick='javascript:window.open("https://www.codecentric.de", "cc")'>click  me</button>

And a simple Robot test with
   Open browser  test.html 
   Click button  bt1
   ${windows}=  Get Window Names
   select window  cc
   sleep  1s
   close window
   ${windows}=  Get Window Names

A "sleep" or "wait until keyword succeeds" after "close window" does not help. 

This is not a Selenium issue. If you code the scenario (open and close a pop-up window) in Java and the Java API for Selenium, everything works fine. No exceptions arise. 

Set-ups:
OS: Mac OS 10.10 and Windows 7
Browsers: Firefox, Chrome, IE10 
Selenium 2.44
Selenium2Library 1.6, 1.5, 1.4

issue_title: Use booleans arguments like in Robot Framework
issue_body:
RF convenience methods for handling booleans, example 
```
| Some Keyword | False |
```
Is evaluated as False in Robot Framework standard libraries, although the argument is string and according Python it should evaluate as True. Changing the S2L to behave in similar fashion as the Robot Framework standard libraries could be useful.

More details in http://robotframework.org/robotframework/latest/libraries/BuiltIn.html#Boolean%20arguments and some discussion in #717

issue_title: Extend xpath detection to support xpath starting with (//
issue_body:
Selenium2Library locators support xpath's that are not prefixed if the xpath starts with `//`. It would also good to support xpath starting with `(//` because it is quite common situation to access index of the xpath. 

Now one has to write
```
| Page Should Contain Element | xpath=(//div)[1] |
```
But it would be nice if this would work:
```
| Page Should Contain Element | (//div)[1] |
```

issue_title: encode_waypoints output differs by Python version
issue_body:
The implementation relies on the behavior of `round` which differs between python 2.7 and 3.x

```
from mapbox.encoding import encode_waypoints

features = [{
  "type": "Feature",
  "geometry": {
    "type": "Point",
    "coordinates": [1, 0]
  },
  "properties": {}}]

print(encode_waypoints(features))
```

gives us
* `1,0` for Python 3
* `1.0,0.0` for Python 2

This should not affect the value returned from the Mapbox SDK but it means that the precise URL is not deterministic - which might affect caching and tests.

issue_title: Post does not work with language_chooser template tag
issue_body:
django cms language_chooser template tag always return current language.

for an example I have blog post that is translated for languages en and ar

I use language_chooser template tag to get translation links while I am in english version of the website:
two links are generated from template tag:
1. en: url points to en version of the post
2. ar: url points to **en** version of the post

I think the problem is with the first two lines of get_absolute_url function which always returns current language:

```
    def get_absolute_url(self, lang=None):
        if not lang or lang not in self.get_available_languages():
            lang = self.get_current_language()
            ...
```

If you comment the above two lines, language_chooser where returns correct url for each language.

issue_title: Clarify & document integration with conda
issue_body:
Looking at the available documentation and repo, I'm struggling to understand how Anaconda/Miniconda fits into `pipenv`. My understanding is that the `conda` distributions can be installed instead/alongside CPython (via `pyenv`). It is not clear how the `conda` and `pipenv` virtual environments work together or interact. 

More importantly, how does `pipenv` interact with `conda`'s `environment.yml` file, does it at all? E.g. can you have a master `Pipfile` which includes `environment.yml` entries (by `pipenv` reading and taking the `environment.yml` into account), or (more likely to be useful) delegate from the `Pipfile` to conda's `environment.yml`? 

This is important to know for mixed environments and eventual migration from `conda` to `pipenv`. To be able to analyse `pipenv` and justify a potential migration, I (and countless others, I suppose ;) ) need to know how these two solutions interact with each other (or if they do at all). Again, this is currently not clear from the available documentation. May you please clarify? 

If no such integration exists, it would be really nice to think about a general "extension" mechanism (in `pipenv` and probably `Pipfile` as well) to integrate other package managers (may it be conda or even cross-language tools like cargo, npm, etc.).

issue_title: Warning not filtered as supposed to
issue_body:
The warning emitted for missing standard script name in [py36compat.py](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/py36compat.py#L76) is not suppressed by [`manifest_maker.warn`](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/egg_info.py#L558) but seems to be supposed to.

I found this while exploring a setup.py as a directory containing a `__main__.py`.  I happened to run

```
python setup.py/__main__.py
```

and I had a warning about the file `__main__.py` missing.

You can suppress the warning by using the `script_name` keyword argument of `setuptools.setup` like so:

```
from setuptools import setup

setup(
    name="mypackage",
    # ... a plethora of other kwargs
    script_name="setup.py"
)
```

issue_title: Package using setup.cfg metadata fails within virtualenv due to outdated setuptools
issue_body:
I'm currently in the process of refactoring a lot of Python code into multiple proper Python packages. What I noticed quickly is that each modules "setup.py" was a slightly modified copy&paste. Fortunately I found that setuptools allows to factor everything variable into a separate setup.cfg:

  https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#configuring-setup-using-setup-cfg-files

The feature needs a setuptools >= December 2016. Not a problem in early 2018 I thought ;-)

When I install the package inside a virtual environment (e.g. to run tox tests) all kinds of odd effects happen. This is a demo of the effect:

```
E:\temp>mkdir venv

E:\temp>c:\Python36\python.exe -m venv venv

E:\temp>venv\Scripts\activate
(venv) E:\temp>pip install e:\Dev\Pype\cstutil\dist\cstutil-0.0.zip
Processing e:\dev\pype\cstutil\dist\cstutil-0.0.zip
Installing collected packages: UNKNOWN
  Running setup.py install for UNKNOWN ... done
Successfully installed UNKNOWN-0.0

(venv) E:\temp>pip install --upgrade setuptools
Collecting setuptools
  Using cached setuptools-38.4.0-py2.py3-none-any.whl
Installing collected packages: setuptools
  Found existing installation: setuptools 28.8.0
    Uninstalling setuptools-28.8.0:
      Successfully uninstalled setuptools-28.8.0
Successfully installed setuptools-38.4.0

(venv) E:\temp>pip install e:\Dev\Pype\cstutil\dist\cstutil-0.0.zip
Processing e:\dev\pype\cstutil\dist\cstutil-0.0.zip
Collecting six (from cstutil==0.0)
  Using cached six-1.11.0-py2.py3-none-any.whl
Requirement already satisfied: setuptools>=30.3.0 in e:\temp\venv\lib\site-packages (from cstutil==0.0)
Installing collected packages: six, cstutil
  Running setup.py install for cstutil ... done
Successfully installed cstutil-0.0 six-1.11.0
```

As you can see the old setuptools version just ignores the metadata. What makes matters worse is that the requirement "setuptools >= 30.3.0" of the package also doesn't work! 

After several "pip install --upgrade setuptools" runs and a lot of scratching my head I discovered that the old setuptools come from within virtualenv.  Is there a chance that the version can be bumped?

Using "install_requires" as parameter in setup.py would allow me to express the setuptools-dependency in a way setuptools 28 understands. Then however setting the (package specific) list of other dependencies would need to go into setup.py again -- defying the whole idea of pushing this information into setup.cfg.

issue_title: support read preferences
issue_body:
txmongo should, like pymongo, fully support read preferences, to permit, for example, failover
to primary, or nearest. Behavior, should be like pymongo 3.2.2 as described here:
https://api.mongodb.org/python/current/api/pymongo/read_preferences.html#module-pymongo.read_preferences.

PRIMARY: Read from the primary. This is the default read preference, and provides the strongest consistency. If no primary is available, raise AutoReconnect.
PRIMARY_PREFERRED: Read from the primary if available, otherwise read from a secondary.
SECONDARY: Read from a secondary. If no matching secondary is available, raise AutoReconnect.
SECONDARY_PREFERRED: Read from a secondary if available, otherwise from the primary.
NEAREST: Read from any available member.

(Note an edge case that should be handled correctly: 
pymongo, when connected to a mongod that happens to be a member of a replica set,
using SECONDARY and the replicaSet option unset, does not failover to other replicaset members.
But this current txmongo code seems to append the members of the set to __allnodes whether or
not replicaSet is set or not. )

If you then decrement again and install the package it again omits these dependencies. I'm yet to fully understand why these two specific packages seem to strongly linked to this package's version.

issue_title: query args order
issue_body:
I know that we've discussed this at least once before, but I think we should reconsider the argument order for criteria and properties for Store.query and Store.query_one.  I think criteria should be first and properties should be second.  As far as I can tell, the main reason we have properties first is because QueryEngine used to, and as far as I can tell no one who is building dbs for MP using maggma has much experience with or love for QueryEngine.

Basically, I want to be able to teach people how to use maggma by appealing to their knowledge of the MPRester query (or if they've got experience with pymongo, pymongo.Collections.find) by saying "this is basically the same as those things", rather than "this is the same as those things, except the argument order is reversed to keep in line with some other thing we used a long time ago".

cppcheck full scan of nupic (09/12/2013), called with these settings:

> Cppcheck is an analysis tool for C/C++ code. Unlike C/C++ compilers and many other analysis tools, we don't detect syntax errors. Cppcheck only detects the types of bugs that the compilers normally fail to detect. The goal is no false positives.

issue_title: NuPIC regression test failure on NAB
issue_body:
You can see this error [here](https://travis-ci.org/numenta/nupic.regression/builds/149548679) and in previous regression test jobs. 

I have run NAB locally and run regression tests locally to get this error on my console, the same one in Travis-CI:

```console
tests/anomaly/nab/nab_anomaly_test.py F

================================================================= FAILURES ==================================================================
___________________________________________________ NABAnomalyTest.testNABScoresBenchmark ___________________________________________________

self = <nab_anomaly_test.NABAnomalyTest testMethod=testNABScoresBenchmark>

    def testNABScoresBenchmark(self):
      """Test that NAB on nupic produces expected scores."""

      with open(BENCHMARK_PATH) as benchmarkFile:
        benchmarkData = json.load(benchmarkFile)

      with open(RESULTS_PATH) as resultsFile:
        resultsData = json.load(resultsFile)

      for benchmarkName in benchmarkData["numenta"]:
        benchmarkValue = benchmarkData["numenta"][benchmarkName]
        resultValue = resultsData["numenta"][benchmarkName]
        self.assertAlmostEqual(benchmarkValue, resultValue, delta=EPS, msg=
            "Numenta detector scores don't check out for the {} profile".
>           format(benchmarkName)
        )
E       AssertionError: Numenta detector scores don't check out for the reward_low_FP_rate profile

tests/anomaly/nab/nab_anomaly_test.py:35: AssertionError
```

issue_title: pyhton3 compatibility
Should self._tieBreaker be removed?
issue_body:
It appears this is no longer used?

https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L330

It used to exist in the ```inhibitColumns()```  method. Is it ok to just remove it in HTM.Java? ...or was there a substitute put in place?

issue_title: Bump version to reflect api changes
issue_body:
I recently ran into problems working on imbu in which some new code depended on some new APIs in KNNClassifier.py (see https://github.com/numenta/numenta-apps/pull/563), and also noticed that some APIs have been removed (see https://github.com/numenta/nupic/commit/837b5fbaef6585982fe3a735c765e8e5661234a8).

Since these are api-breaking changes, we should bump the version.

cc @rhyolight

issue_title: CLAClassifier compute() parameter documentation needs changing.
issue_body:
The parameter documentation describes the nature of the inputs to the compute method. The current description for ```patternNZ``` should be added to, to specify that when input is from a **temporal_memory.py**, it should be the indexes of **active cells**, not just say **active indexes**. 
This author payed a somewhat high price for the lack of disambiguation.

issue_title: TM.learnOnSegments: Create activeSynapses Set only when needed
issue_body:
Simple oversight...

``` Python
if self.predictedSegmentDecrement > 0:
      for segment in prevMatchingSegments:
        isPredictedInactiveCell = connections.cellForSegment(segment) in predictedInactiveCells
        activeSynapses = self.activeSynapsesForSegment(
          segment, prevActiveCells, connections)

        if isPredictedInactiveCell:
          self.adaptSegment(segment, activeSynapses, connections,
                            -self.predictedSegmentDecrement,
                            0.0)
```

In the code above, "activeSynapses" is only used when "isPredictedInactiveCell" is true, therefore its creation should happen inside the if statement like this:

``` Python
if self.predictedSegmentDecrement > 0:
      for segment in prevMatchingSegments:
        isPredictedInactiveCell = connections.cellForSegment(segment) in predictedInactiveCells
        
        if isPredictedInactiveCell:
          activeSynapses = self.activeSynapsesForSegment(
            segment, prevActiveCells, connections)
          self.adaptSegment(segment, activeSynapses, connections,
                            -self.predictedSegmentDecrement,
                            0.0)
```

This would protect against creation of unused sets.

issue_title: Move Connections class from TemporalMemory to a separate module
issue_body:
As SpatialPooler and other classes can re-use Connections class, it's interesting that this class be called from a specific file than `temporal_memory.py`, i.e. `connections.py`.

Furthermore, in order to make this class more abstract, also is interesting we change the `cell` term in methods like `cellForSegment` to something like `element` because an element can be a cell (in case of distal connections) or a collumn (in case of proximal connections) which both has segments. By the way, I'm open to suggestions.

issue_title: add support for monitor mixins to write charts to file instead of open new window
issue_body: The mixins currently open matplotlib chart windows but this isn't always desired and doesn't work when running inside a GUI application. The mixins should support an option for writing the output to a file instead of opening the charts in a window.

issue_title: Implement Network capnp serialization
issue_body: See parent #441

issue_title: optimize for x86/64 CPUs rather than Neo15
issue_body:
There are some optimizations for `Neo15` (the mobile phone?) CPU, I think we should rather focus on common `x86/x64` CPU most of us are using.

When compiling interfaces into shared objects, `zope` cannot resolve them correctly. For an SSCCE, the compiled example interface from https://docs.zope.org/zope.interface/README.html raises

```python
    raise InvalidInterface("Concrete attribute, " + name)
zope.interface.exceptions.InvalidInterface: Concrete attribute, bar
```

I collected the procedure in a bash script (https://gist.github.com/hoefling/c113a46f1690118ef4bde3114b2960c7). It generates a python module containing the interface and a test script that tries to import that interface:

issue_title: Enhancement: Get domain for email from request
issue_body:
We've got 100's of tenants running out of a single settings.py file.  Having settings.DOMAIN provided in settings.py is pretty limiting.  Suggestion would be to pull the domain from the request.  There may be a better way though.

Here is the change I made to utils.py

        def get_email_context(self, user):
            token = self.token_generator.make_token(user)
            uid = encode_uid(user.pk)
            # Pull domain from request
            try:
                domain = self.request.get_host()
            except:
                domain = settings.get('DOMAIN')
            return {
                'user': user,
                'domain': domain, # Use domain from request or if not provided fall back to settings.
                'site_name': settings.get('SITE_NAME'),
                'uid': uid,
                'token': token,
                'protocol': 'https' if self.request.is_secure() else 'http',
            }

issue_title: Local reference not freed if JavaMethod is called with array as argument and argtype is Ljava/lang/Object;
issue_body:
Example code:
```python
Array = autoclass("java.lang.reflect.Array")
Array.set([None], 0, None)
```

The argument ```[None]``` gets converted to an java object because the argtype is ```Ljava/lang/Object;``` and ```py_arg``` is ```list```:
```cython
cdef void populate_args(JNIEnv *j_env, tuple definition_args, jvalue *j_args, args) except *:
...
        elif argtype[0] == 'L':
...
            elif isinstance(py_arg, (tuple, list)):
                j_args[index].l = convert_pyarray_to_java(j_env, argtype, py_arg)
```

But the local reference is not freed:
```cython
cdef void release_args(JNIEnv *j_env, tuple definition_args, jvalue *j_args, args) except *:
...
        if argtype[0] == 'L':
            if py_arg is None:
                j_args[index].l = NULL
            if isinstance(py_arg, basestring) and \
                    argtype in (
                        'Ljava/lang/String;',
                        'Ljava/lang/CharSequence;',
                        'Ljava/lang/Object;'):
                j_env[0].DeleteLocalRef(j_env, j_args[index].l)
```
You forgot the case that ```py_arg``` is ```tuple``` or ```list```.

issue_title: Pyjnius class mocking
issue_body:
Hello,

I am using pyjnius in a project. I am attempting of mocking classes generated by pyjnius, created through `autoclass` and `cast`. I'm using [pytest-mock](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-mock), which is just a wrapper of [unittest's mock](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html). At this point I'm stuck in a possible solution:

pytest-mock requires the fully qualified python class name that is to be mocked. However, as the python class (generated by pyjnius) is generated at runtime, pytest-mock cannot access it. I think that this problem is ortogonal to the mocking framework itself, as I can't explicitly reference the generated class either (through, for instance, `jnius.reflect.generated.MyClass()`). The usual error is:
`AttributeError: module 'jnius.reflect' has no attribute 'generated'`

Has anyone tried this before? Is there any documentation that I'm missing?

I can't find any references that justify these changes in the NDK, but they only seem to be in API 21. `r10d` and `r10e` both have the same issue.

issue_title: NE3: Support AArch64
issue_body:
`pip install blosc` on aarch64 / python3.5.2 errors out due to cpuinfo.py not having a match clause for aarch64.

This is solvable by adding the following three lines:

```
+       elif re.match('^aarch64$', raw_arch_string):
+               arch = 'AARCH_64'
+               bits = 64
```

Cheers!
Ian

issue_title: race condition: sys.stdout faster than sys.stderr
issue_body:
Calls to write to stdout and stderr are not ouput to the console in the same sequence as they are called. The call to stdout often overtakes the call to stderr.
This code:
    from sys import stdout, stderr
    stderr.write("writing to stderr\n")
    stdout.write("writing to stdout\n")
prints
    writing to stdout
    writing to stderr
The same happens for this code:
    print >> stderr, "writing to stderr"
    print "writing to stdout"
Note that the sequence is reversed.
This is on Win 7 with Spyder 3.1.4 and Python 2.7, both in the Python and IPython consoles. It can't be reproduced in IDLE.

issue_title: Unexpected parsing behaviour for --date=2018-12-09 vs. --date=2018-12-10
issue_body:
Anything that can be interpreted as an arithmetics expression will be magically "calculated" by fire. This is very practical. But it bit me because I wanted to parse dates from command line parameters using fire, see below. 

Suggestion: accept type hints as a way to prevent this. A parameter with type hint "str" shoud not be "calculated". 

Test code:

```import fire

def test(string: str=None) :
    print(string)

if __name__=="__main__" :
    fire.Fire(test)
```

Will give you: 

```
> python ./test-parsing.py --string=2010-10-10
1990

> python ./test-parsing.py --string=2010-10-09
2010-10-09

```

issue_title: Exception Fingerprints
issue_body:
I'd like to customize exception grouping, and it sounds like this is possible using fingerprints. However, the fingerprints need to be tagged at capture time or set on the context. I'd like to tag exceptions with a fingerprint at the raise site, because thats really the only place it can reasonably know how to group itself.

We could potentially set an attribute/property `fingerprint` that the capture code could check for, or try to call a `get_fingerprint()` or somesuch?

issue_title: SWC parser: split soma neurites have parent sections.
issue_body:
NeuroM treats neurites and somata separately. Neurites are disconnected trees and somata are collections or points. Thus the root node of a neurite should have parent `None`, and NeuroM code relies on this. It seems the SWC parser assigns soma parent sections to some neurites in data with split soma points, i.e. data where the soma points are not consecutive.

To reproduce:

```
import neurom as nm
n = nm.load_neuron('./test_data/swc/contour_split_soma_neuron.swc')
for nt in n.neurites:
    print nt.root_node.parent is None
```

Desired output:

```
True
True
True
```

Observed output:

```
True
False
False
```

This is probably the reason behind #438.

issue_title: Soma.center as a class property
issue_body:
Currently, the center of the soma is set when the soma is created and its value is reflected in the list of the soma points. If its value is changed, the respective point in the point list is not changed as well which leads into having to take into account both the points and the soma center for rotations, translations.

Wouldn't it be more convenient if center was a soma property (using @property decorator) and not a variable which is set upon initialisation? This way any changes in the soma points will affect the soma.center as well.

Moreover, the soma center is a tuple while everything are numpy arrays which forces me to explicitly check for a tuple type for the transformations.

issue_title: Demoted node does not start on fail over when using synchronous mode
issue_body:
I am testing 3 nodes in a cluster with  synchronous mode.

```
patronictl -c /etc/patroni/baruwa.yml list baruwa
+---------+------------------------------+--------------+--------+---------+-----------+
| Cluster | Member                       | Host         |  Role  |  State  | Lag in MB |
+---------+------------------------------+--------------+--------+---------+-----------+
| baruwa  | db.home.topdog-software.com  | 192.168.1.15 | Leader | running |       0.0 |
| baruwa  | db2.home.topdog-software.com | 192.168.1.33 |        | running |       0.0 |
| baruwa  | db3.home.topdog-software.com | 192.168.1.34 |        | running |       0.0 |
+---------+------------------------------+--------------+--------+---------+-----------+
```

When i trigger a manual failover using `patronictl -c /etc/patroni/baruwa.yml failover baruwa` The fail over happens and a different host takes over as the leader. However the previous leader is not able to startup postgresql as the `synchronous_standby_names` option is not removed from `postgresql.conf` file.

If i stop the patroni process and start it up again, the `postgresql.conf` file is updated removing the `synchronous_standby_names` option allowing postgresql to startup and rejoin the cluster.

issue_title: Add flag for validation disabling
issue_body:
In my swagger definition I have a lot of parameters which contain a valid datatype which then is needed for our automated query builder:

```yaml
  PropertyGroup:
    properties:
      time_start:
        description: Start time of flare
        type: string
        format: date-time

      nar:
        description: NOAA active region number
        type: number
        format: int32

      lat_hg:
        description: Stonyhurst heliographic latitude of event
        type: number
        format: int32
```

Now the problem we encounter is, that sometimes the validation of these types is not possible because we need for example **nullable types** as well.

So my question is, if it is possible to add an option which disables the validation or a method to provide an own validator class for some datatypes?

issue_title: Convert camelcased parameter names to Python style convention
issue_body:
### Description
It's common to define Swagger parameter names in camelcase ("postalCode", "modifiedDate", "statusCode", etc.), but this goes against Python PEP-8 convention.

### Expected behaviour

From Swagger:

    get:
      parameters:
        - name: postalCode
          in: query
          type: string

I would like to be able to write

    def search(postal_code, ...):
        ...

### Actual behaviour

The parameters are not found:

    TypeError: search() missing 1 required positional argument: 'postal_code'


### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

$ python --version
Python 3.5.2
$ pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"
Version: 1.0.129


Here's a regex for the conversion: http://stackoverflow.com/a/1176023/594211

issue_title: Recipe: Capture exceptions during celery/kombu's serialization
issue_body:
Example for pickle; works similar for JSON, YAML, …
```python
def capture_serialization_exceptions():
    from pickle import dumps

    from kombu import serialization
    from kombu.exceptions import DecodeError
    from opbeat.contrib.django.models import client

    def loads(s):
        try:
            return serialization.unpickle(s)
        except LookupError as e:
            client.capture_exception()
            raise
        except DecodeError as e:
            client.capture_exception()
            raise
        except Exception as e:
            client.capture_exception()
            raise

    # See https://github.com/celery/kombu/blob/v3.0.37/kombu/serialization.py#L342-L360
    serialization.register(
        name='my_pickle',
        encoder=dumps,
        decoder=loads,
        content_type='application/x-python-serialize',
        content_encoding='binary',
)
```

I did not figure out where to put this.  If you can point me to a file, I'm happy to open a PR.

I guess the `LookupError` is enough, but we did not care enough to investigate.  If someone from opbeat wants to see a logged example (closed source project; with a LookupError), I'm happy to provide a link via email.

We call the functions when doing https://opbeat.com/docs/articles/get-started-with-django/#logging-celery-tasks

issue_title: GNU screen issue when multiple tabs are created from script and Guake starts hidden
issue_body:
So I have a script in autostart which creates 6 tabs in Guake and launches few diagnostic programs in 4 of them (`htop`, `vnstat`, `netstat` and `i7z`). Because `vnstat` and `i7z`’s output can be fit in less than half of one tab, instead of running them in separate tabs I tried to run `screen` with custom screenrc file that would split terminal into 2 panes and execute each command in its own pane. It worked – well, sort of.

All behaves as expected when mentioned script is executed when Guake window is visible. When it is not, output of `vnstat` and `i7z` inside `screen` appears few lines below, almost in the center:

issue_title: Hangs when target do not respond
issue_body:
In http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#timeouts, it is said that an explicit timeout has to be passed into requests in order to get a timeout. Currently memento-client do not allow a timeout to be passed into [in any way](https://github.com/mementoweb/py-memento-client/blob/c7d3d1c35eba2e4dd219d01a5078bf5b18e923bb/memento_client/memento_client.py#L358). This causes issues when the server the script is accessing do not respond:
```
$ strace python3 -c 'import datetime, memento_client; mc = memento_client.MementoClient(); mc.timegate_uri = "http://web.archive.org/web/"; mc.get_memento_info("http://www.icann.org/en/tlds/agreements/cat/cat-agreement-23sep05.htm", datetime.datetime.now())'
[...]
read(4, "\1\0\0\0\0\0\0\0\327\374U'nn\232\331\212n\35\331$\tM\nd\200\305[H\324=\272"..., 40) = 40
ioctl(4, FIONBIO, [0])                  = 0
write(4, "\27\3\3\0\331\232\217\f!\7\303u\241\375)F\365\334\332*\222\255O\22\272\201\252\226\274ru\203"..., 222) = 222
ioctl(4, FIONBIO, [0])                  = 0
read(4, 
```
`ioctl(4, FIONBIO, [0])` sets blocking network IO and `read(4,` will block for as long as we are concerned.

Please provide a default timeout or allow users to specify their own timeout.

(This is part of an [issue](https://phabricator.wikimedia.org/T185561) reported downstream.)

issue_title: Supporting aiobotocore
issue_body:
Back again with more async stuff :smile:

I've been using the aiobotocore library which essentially wraps botocore and uses aiohttp to perform async http requests.

I was looking into patching parts of it so the SDK creates subsegments when queries to AWS are made.
I've gotten it to work but its a bit messy. 

The following is based off the botocore patcher. (The injecting headers bit is exactly the same bar a different class needs patching)

```python
    wrapt.wrap_function_wrapper(
        'aiobotocore.client',
        'AioBaseClient._make_api_call',
        _xray_traced_aiobotocore,
    )

async def _xray_traced_aiobotocore(wrapped, instance, args, kwargs):
    service = instance._service_model.metadata["endpointPrefix"]

    return await xray_recorder.record_subsegment_async(
        wrapped, instance, args, kwargs,
        name=service,
        namespace='aws',
        meta_processor=aws_meta_processor,
    )
```
When wrapt runs, the wrapped service is a coroutine which needs to be awaited.
Until its awaited, the injecting of headers wont run.

I created a copy of `record_subsegment` called `record_subsegment_async` which is also a coroutine and it runs  `return_value = await wrapped(*args, **kwargs)` which during that the headers are injected and then the result is returned. After that everything else works.

I tried awaiting the wrapped function inside of `_xray_traced_aiobotocore` but when the headers are injected the subsegment hasn't begun as thats done inside of `record_subsegment`.

What would you reckon is the best way for solving this, I was thinking of subclassing xray_recorder but if there is a simpler way I'm up for that. Then another PR will come your way :smile:.

issue_title: conv2d_bias performance issues
issue_body:
Hi, I replaced the LSTM in a classifier with the following class:
```
class conv():
    def __init__(self, model, n_gram, filter_size, emb_size):
        self.emb_size = emb_size
        self.filter = model.add_parameters((n_gram, filter_size, embeddings_size, embeddings_size))
        self.bias = model.add_parameters((embeddings_size))
        
    def __call__(self, embeds):
        embeds = dy.concatenate_cols(embeds)
        return dy.rectify(dy.conv2d_bias(embeds, self.filter, self.bias, [1, 1], is_valid=False))
```

The LSTM based network ~6 hours while the CONV run for ~7 hours,
As I understand  CONV should be faster.

Is it known? 

Thanks, Tal 👍🏻

*The accuracy of the classifier also suffered but this was sort of expected.

issue_title: Left multiplication of a matrix by a vector
issue_body:
DyNet currently supports matrix-vector multiplication, but does not support vector-matrix multiplication.

For example, we can multiply a 2x3 matrix and a length-3 vector to obtain a length-2 vector,
```
>>> import dynet as dy
>>> v = dy.inputVector([0, 0, 0])
>>> w = dy.inputMatrix([0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0], (2, 3))
>>> result = w * v
>>> result.npvalue()
array([ 0.,  0.])
```
but we cannot multiply a length-2 vector and a 2x3 matrix to obtain a length-3 vector,
```
>>> import dynet as dy
>>> v = dy.inputVector([0, 0])
>>> w = dy.inputMatrix([0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0], (2, 3))
>>> result = v * w
libc++abi.dylib: terminating with uncaught exception of type std::invalid_argument: Mismatched input dimensions in MatrixMultiply: [{2} {2,3}]
Abort trap: 6
```

Using `dynet.transpose`, we can write
```
>>> import dynet as dy
>>> v = dy.inputVector([0, 0])
>>> w = dy.inputMatrix([0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0], (2, 3))
>>> result = dy.transpose(w) * v
>>> result.npvalue()
array([ 0.,  0.,  0.])
```
but `dynet/expr.h` mentions that `transpose` is an O(n) operation, so this seems like it would be an inefficient workaround.

Alternatively, we can also write
```
>>> import dynet as dy
>>> v = dy.inputVector([0, 0])
>>> w = dy.inputMatrix([0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0], (2, 3))
>>> result = dy.transpose(v) * w
>>> result.npvalue()
array([[ 0.,  0.,  0.]])
```
but this returns a 1x3 matrix instead of a length-3 vector. We could then use `dynet.reshape` to turn this back into a vector, but that requires knowing the length of the vector, which may be inconvenient.

Would it be possible to add support for this? Or does the existing API already support vector-matrix multiplication by other means?

issue_title: delete_dim does not work as expected
issue_body:
Hi, When implementing dimension-wise mean, I find function: delete_dim in dim.h may not work as expected. 
If a tensor's dim is 
`Dim({2, 5, 7}, 10)`
and argument of delete_dim function `i` is 2, which means I wanna reduce a tensor with 4 rank(including batch dimension) along the third dimension. The expected result is Dim({2, 5}, 10), but in fact delete_dim 
returns Dim({2, 5, 7}, 10) , since the loop requires `i+1<nd`
Besides, in some scenarios, the batch dimension may also need to be reduced, but current delete_dim cannot handle it. Maybe we need a new delete_dim ,which can delete multi dimensions in one call and delete batch dimension when necessary.

        self.client = storage.Client()
        self.currentBucket  = None
        self.currentBlob    = None

    def get_or_create_bucket(self, bucketCfg):
        bucketName = bucketCfg.get('name')
        self.currentBucket = self.client.get_bucket(bucketName)
        return self.currentBucket

    def get_or_create_blob(self, blobCfg, bucket=None):        
        if not bucket:  bucket              = self.currentBucket
        else:           self.currentBucket  = bucket

        blobName = blobCfg.get('blobName')
        
        blob = bucket.get_blob(blobName)
        if blob is None:
            blob = bucket.blob(blobName) #, chunksize, int, multiple of 256k

        self.currentBlob = blob
        return blob

    def blob_exists(self, blobCfg, bucket=None):        
        if not bucket:  bucket              = self.currentBucket
        else:           self.currentBucket  = bucket

        blobName = blobCfg.get('blobName')
        
        blob = bucket.get_blob(blobName)
        return blob is not None
        
    def upload_file(self, blobCfg, blob=None):
        if not blob:    blob                = self.currentBlob
        else:           self.currentBlob    = blob
    
        filename = blobCfg.get('localFilename')

        startTime = datetime.now()
        res = blob.upload_from_filename(filename=filename)

        return (datetime.now() - startTime).total_seconds()

    def gs_uri(self, blob):
        return "gs://{}/{}".format(blob.bucket.name, blob.name)



client = StorageClient()

blobCfg     = blobsCfg.get(sys.argv[1])
if blobCfg is None: usage('No such FileID')

bucket  = client.get_or_create_bucket   ( bucketCfg )
if client.blob_exists(blobCfg): fail('Blob already exists')

blob    = client.get_or_create_blob     (   blobCfg )
res     = client.upload_file            (   blobCfg )
`

issue_title: cors: true
issue_body:
One thing that I like about serverless.com is that CORS can be enabled for an endpoint in one short line:

    cors: true

Currently, to enable CORS using `x-amazon-apigateway-integration`, it is necessary to:

- add a header to each response:

```yaml
responses:
        '200':
          description: Request successful
          headers:
            Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
              type: string
          schema:
            $ref: '#/definitions/Success'
        '400':
          description: Authentication failed
          headers:
            Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
              type: string
          schema:
            $ref: '#/definitions/Error'
```

- add responseParameters in `x-amazon-apigateway-integration`:

```yaml
default:
            statusCode: "200"
            responseParameters:
              method.response.header.Access-Control-Allow-Origin : "'*'"
          '.*"status":400.*':
            statusCode: '400'
            responseParameters:
              method.response.header.Access-Control-Allow-Origin : "'*'"
```

- add a massive `options` section:
```yaml
options:
      summary: CORS support
      description: |
        Enable CORS by returning correct headers
      responses:
        200:
          description: Default response for CORS method
          headers:
            Access-Control-Allow-Headers:
              type: string
            Access-Control-Allow-Methods:
              type: string
            Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
              type: string
      x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
        type: mock
        requestTemplates:
          application/json: |
            {"statusCode" : 200}
        responses:
          "default":
            statusCode: "200"
            responseParameters:
              method.response.header.Access-Control-Allow-Headers : "'Content-Type,X-Amz-Date,Authorization,X-Api-Key'"
              method.response.header.Access-Control-Allow-Methods : "'*'"
              method.response.header.Access-Control-Allow-Origin : "'*'"
            responseTemplates:
              application/json: |
                {}
```

...it'd be great if `Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31` could transform the swagger schema, automatically adding all of this if `cors: true` is present in the CloudFormation object

issue_title: Custom Authorizer Function ARN within Swagger File
issue_body:
We have the following scenario: 

We want with single Cloud Formation stack to deploy an API Gateway having methods secured with Lambda based Custom Authorizer. The API must be defined with Swagger file.

The Custom Authorizer Lambda must be created within the same Cloud Formation stack.  The question is if or how we can reference the Custom Authorizer Lambda function within the Swagger file?

For example:

```
securityDefinitions:
  OktaCustomAuthorizer:
    type: "apiKey"
    name: "Authorization"
    in: "header"
    x-amazon-apigateway-authtype: "custom"
    x-amazon-apigateway-authorizer:
      authorizerResultTtlInSeconds: 300
      authorizerUri: "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/<<FUNCTION_ARN>>/invocations"
      type: "token"
```

If Authorizer Lambda have been created previously, we can statically reference it in the construct above. However, what if Custom Authorizer function is part of the current stack? The API method Lambda request integrations can handle these kind of scenarios with stage variables substitutions, i.e. ${stageVariables.LambdaFunctionName}. But for Custom Authorizers it is not the case.

Any opinion? The requirement for having everything in one stack seems very reasonable.

Best Regards,

issue_title: Support for pending transactions?
issue_body:
It seems support for pending transactions must pass through to the `/connect` endpoint as per the docs:

![http://i.nick.sg/image/321k2Q0g353N/Image%202015-11-30%20at%202.05.46%20PM.png](http://i.nick.sg/image/321k2Q0g353N/Image%202015-11-30%20at%202.05.46%20PM.png)

However, passing this option like this does not appear to return pending transactions:

```
response = client.connect('usaa', {
    'username': 'user',
    'password': 'pass',
    'pin': '1234'
}, options={
    'pending': True
})
```

This Python lib seems to want the `pending` arg on `connect_get` here: https://github.com/plaid/plaid-python/blob/master/plaid/client.py#L390, but that doesn't appear to work, either.

Not sure if this issue is with `plaid-python`'s implementation or an issue with the API.

issue_title: mimwrite breaks if input is a generator
issue_body:
mimwrite breaks if ims is a generator because of this section:
```
    #Ensure non empty sequence
    if len(ims) == 0:
        raise ValueError('Numpy sequence must not be empty.')
```
since generators don't have a __len__ attribute. 

If it's necessary to check for empty sequence, [this workaround](https://stackoverflow.com/a/664239/6038094) could be used for generator types.

issue_title: Some Windows test builds are failing to install lxml
issue_body:
Appveyor has been angry about lxml every since we [bumped the requirement from 3.6.0 to 3.7.3 in parserator](https://github.com/datamade/parserator/commit/0963dd444fd665fa4b93c16a9932892a0ff198a7) and seems to be unable to install it. We're getting variations of [this error message] (https://ci.appveyor.com/project/fgregg/usaddress/build/1.0.50/job/ovarsc9aqjsgxbfw#L129) in builds for Python 3.4: 

```
C:\Users\appveyor\AppData\Local\Temp\1\xmlXPathInitk413vky1.c(1) : fatal error C1083: Cannot open include file: 'libxml/xpath.h': No such file or directory
    *********************************************************************************
    Could not find function xmlCheckVersion in library libxml2. Is libxml2 installed?
    *********************************************************************************
```

The error seems to happen when Appveyor has to build lxml from source ([sample here, Python 3.4 on x64](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/fgregg/usaddress/build/1.0.50/job/a5a1f3atkm1iodt4)). When it builds a wheel instead ([as it does for Python 2.7](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/fgregg/usaddress/build/1.0.50/job/26c1crepo77ld6fk#L27)) everything runs smoothly. Seems like there are no wheels for Python 3.4 and Windows architectures [on PyPi.](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/lxml/3.7.3)

issue_title: AJAX error when logged out because of idle session
issue_body:
## Summary

Currently, the automatic logout (session idle time) kicks in after 5 minutes. Issue #2389 is addressing if this should be upped.

Regardless, we shouldn't be showing AJAX errors to users that are logged out. Case in question:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/374612/31355230-b8d8ffb2-ad39-11e7-8fe8-7f0a6cc1da44.png)

The full error:

```
{ "request": { "path": "/api/contentsessionlog/0f60bd08465cb59a647677e631cafa41/", "method": "PATCH", "entity": "{\"channel_id\":\"8b4d3e6d3d4842ba8ea658335b5dd252\",\"content_id\":\"a973b96308c053f781bdcb51ec3563b2\",\"kind\":\"exercise\",\"start_timestamp\":\"2017-10-09T19:24:38.393Z\",\"end_timestamp\":\"2017-10-09T19:34:52.047Z\",\"time_spent\":608.3469999999996,\"progress\":0,\"extra_fields\":\"{}\",\"user\":\"7cc57c59baf63db55726b2c264766f8e\",\"pk\":\"0f60bd08465cb59a647677e631cafa41\"}", "headers": { "X-CSRFToken": "aYMYM3COfdBimconahGJyQpCp3SMG2QG", "Content-Type": "application/json", "Accept": "application/json, application/json;q=0.8, text/plain;q=0.5, */*;q=0.2" }, "canceled": false }, "url": "/api/contentsessionlog/0f60bd08465cb59a647677e631cafa41/", "raw": {}, "status": { "code": 403, "text": "Forbidden" }, "headers": { "Allow": "GET, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS", "Content-Type": "application/json", "Date": "Mon, 09 Oct 2017 19:34:52 GMT", "Server": "0.0.0.0", "Transfer-Encoding": "chunked", "Vary": "Accept, Cookie", "X-Frame-Options": "SAMEORIGIN" }, "entity": { "detail": "Authentication credentials were not provided." } } 
```

Btw I'm not sure if it's kosher to dump the CSRF token in a text that you expect people to share -- okay okay this is an offline app, but I mean we might end up copying the code to use it on an online site (and it does run in online demos). In Django, there is a filter on tracebacks etc. that's sent by email and displayed when `DEBUG=True`.


I've added 0.4 as a milestone for this, question is if we can easily solve it for 0.4 or if it's easier to solve in a later version? @indirectlylit ?

issue_title: Add ability to whitelist checks we're ok with failing

issue_body:
The etree serializer breaks if an Entity object is present. To reproduce: import html5lib from html5lib.serializer import HTMLSerializer import lxml from lxml import etree def render(element, treeType, implementation=None): walker_cls = html5lib.getTreeWalker(treeType, implementation=implementation) walker = walker_cls(element) serializer = HTMLSerializer() print(serializer.render(walker)) elements = html5lib.parseFragment('
', treebuilder='lxml') element, = elements element.append(etree.Entity('amp')) # Works, outputs: #
&
render(element, 'lxml') # Fails with the exception below. render(element, 'etree', implementation=lxml.etree) Exception: Traceback (most recent call last): File "test-html5lib.py", line 25, in render(element, 'etree', implementation=lxml.etree) File "test-html5lib.py", line 16, in render print(serializer.render(walker)) File "/.../python3.6/site-packages/html5lib/serializer.py", line 323, in render return "".join(list(self.serialize(treewalker))) File "/.../python3.6/site-packages/html5lib/serializer.py", line 209, in serialize for token in treewalker: File "/.../python3.6/site-packages/html5lib/filters/optionaltags.py", line 18, in __iter__ for previous, token, next in self.slider(): File "/.../python3.6/site-packages/html5lib/filters/optionaltags.py", line 9, in slider for token in self.source: File "/.../python3.6/site-packages/html5lib/treewalkers/base.py", line 94, in __iter__ details = self.getNodeDetails(currentNode) File "/.../python3.6/site-packages/html5lib/treewalkers/etree.py", line 61, in getNodeDetails assert isinstance(node.tag, string_types), type(node.tag) AssertionError: I believe this is because etree.py's `TreeWalker` class's [`getNodeDetails()` method](https://github.com/html5lib/html5lib-python/blob/984f934d30ba2fde0e9ce5d4581c73fb81654474/html5lib/treewalkers/etree.py#L39) does not have logic like the [following in `etree_lxml.py`](https://github.com/html5lib/html5lib-python/blob/984f934d30ba2fde0e9ce5d4581c73fb81654474/html5lib/treewalkers/etree_lxml.py#L155): elif node.tag == etree.Entity: return base.ENTITY, ensure_str(node.text)[1:-1] # strip &;

issue_title: Unprotected submitted files and pictures in filer_public - make use of diavo/django-filer
issue_body:
Currently all files and pictures transmitted through aldryn-forms are stored somewhere within the media/filer_public folder, thus availabe unprotected to the public / everyone who knows or guesses the randomly created link.

Since django-filer is a requirement of aldryn-forms already and already capable of protecting files, I strongly suggest to make both addons collaborate to their full potential.

When installing the aldryn-forms addon or even better at the individual form level, a choice should be given to have the submitted data protected from public access, i.e. accessable only when logged-in - this is cruicial for many use cases with critical data!

issue_title: Allow CDXJ piping from the indexer to replay
issue_body:
The current default behavior for the indexer is to print the resulting CDXJ to stdout. Ideally, this should be able to be piped to the replay script and used as a basis CDXJ instead of relying on an external resource. This would remove the step of needing to write to disk, and potentially speed up the process.

Though this can probably be done more elegantly through inter-process communication, a good first step might be just to allow:

`ipwb index myCaptures.warc | ipwb replay`

and have replay use stdin as a fallback if no other source for the archive index is provided (or maybe, even if one is).

```python
})
```

issue_title: discussion around utilizing config.yaml options
issue_body:
One thing I'm noticing while developing layers is that while the config.yaml options bubble up to the topmost layer you can run into issues where config options may get merged if their keys are the same. For example, a `port` which could be a setting in an `nginx` layer, and a setting in `yourapplication` layer if setup to utilize the reverseproxy.

The way i've currently dealt with it is to prefix my config options, so for example, nginx I have `nginx-port`, and my web application will then have `mywebapp-port`.

Depending on the amount of layers you have this could lead to more headache trying to manage those config options from several layers. This should probably rarely be an issue as the amount of layers used per charm would be minimal I thought it'd be something to think about for documentation.

issue_title: Optionally accept (warn) local charms in bundles
issue_body: For CI, bundles may have one or more charms replaced with local builds of those charms for testing.  However, we'd like to still be able to proof the bundle, since that's a standard part of bundletester.  To support that, we'd need the [`charm.is_local()`](https://github.com/juju/juju-bundlelib/blob/master/jujubundlelib/validation.py#L192) check issue a warning instead of an error.

issue_title: Factor reduce scope and variable ordering
issue_body:
**Problem 1: factor reduction makes an assumption on the order in which variables are passed in:**

Original factor:
╒═════╤═════╤════════════╕
│ 3   │ 2   │   phi(3,2) │
╞═════╪═════╪════════════╡
│ 3_0 │ 2_0 │     0.3900 │
├─────┼─────┼────────────┤
│ 3_0 │ 2_1 │     0.0600 │
├─────┼─────┼────────────┤
│ 3_1 │ 2_0 │     0.6100 │
├─────┼─────┼────────────┤
│ 3_1 │ 2_1 │     0.9400 │
╘═════╧═════╧════════════╛
Observed (2_0, 3_1):
╒═════════╕
│   phi() │
╞═════════╡
│  0.0600 │
╘═════════╛

Expected output: 0.61. The problem occurs because the evidence was passed in as (2_0, 3_1) instead of (3_1, 2_0).

**Problem 2: when a variable is passed in that is not in scope, an error occurs.**

Original factor:
╒═════╤═════╤════════════╕
│ 2   │ 1   │   phi(2,1) │
╞═════╪═════╪════════════╡
│ 2_0 │ 1_0 │     0.5900 │
├─────┼─────┼────────────┤
│ 2_0 │ 1_1 │     0.2200 │
├─────┼─────┼────────────┤
│ 2_1 │ 1_0 │     0.4100 │
├─────┼─────┼────────────┤
│ 2_1 │ 1_1 │     0.7800 │
╘═════╧═════╧════════════╛

Observed (2_0, 3_1):

ERROR: test_observe_evidence

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "../pgmpy/factors/Factor.py", line 276, in reduce
    raise Exceptions.ScopeError('%s not in scope' % list(set(reduced_variables)-set(factor.scope())))
pgmpy.exceptions.Exceptions.ScopeError: "['3'] not in scope"

The error occurs because '3' is not in scope of the factor.

Problem 2 doesn't happen when the factor is used through one of the inference methods (variable elimination or belief propagation). I'm not sure about problem 1. However, in either case the implementation now couples the Factor class to the VE / BP implementations. If that's the intended use, i.e. if Factor shouldn't be used on its own, it should be a private class.

I propose to fix the above in the Factor class. It's not a difficult fix and shouldn't have performance impact.

issue_title: newapi / component API accept session_factory as a keyword
issue_body:
```
class Redacted(ApplicationSession):
    s = None
    protocol = None

if __name__ == '__main__':

    args = parser.parse_args()

    extra = vars(args)
    url = extra.pop('url')
    realm = extra.pop('realm')
    ### Inserted code goes here ###
    run(component)
```
Given the above code, adding the following code *should* work but doesn't: 
```
    component = Component(
        transports=url
        session_factory = Redacted
        realm=realm,
        extra=extra
    )
```

However, what works is: 
```
    component = Component(
        transports=url,
        realm=realm,
        extra=extra
    )
    component.session_factory = Redacted
```

I believe that the property `session_factory` should be added as a kwarg, and @meejah asked me to submit this issue

issue_title: store: consider switching filesystem to XFS
issue_body:
From http://ceph.com/docs/master/rados/configuration/filesystem-recommendations/#filesystems

> Ceph OSD Daemons depend on the Extended Attributes (XATTRs) of the underlying file system for various forms of internal object state and metadata. The underlying filesystem must provide sufficient capacity for XATTRs. btrfs does not bound the total xattr metadata stored with a file. XFS has a relatively large limit (64 KB) that most deployments won’t encounter, but the ext4 is too small to be usable.


Following is this:

> You should always add the following line to the [osd] section of your ceph.conf file for ext4 filesystems; you can optionally use it for btrfs and XFS.:

```
filestore xattr use omap = true
```

We switched from btrfs to ext4 in #3319 and didn't add the `filestore xattr use omap` configuration directive. We should either switch this volume to use XFS (probably preferred) or add this configuration, although it is unknown how this would affect existing clusters on upgrade. 

Note also that we should split out the `daemon` and `monitor` volumes anyhow - that is tracked as #3124.

issue_title: Rewrite EC2 scripts (again) to remove CloudFormation
issue_body:
CloudFormation just isn't doing the job for us. Specifically, the major issues are:
- We can't determine which availability zones in a region support subnets (see #1601)
- Editing the template to remove resources when we're launching into an existing VPC is hacky and error-prone (see #1684)

We should just have the provision script use the AWS CLI to create each resource manually. We'll also need a cleanup script to destroy the cluster.

issue_title: Calculation of Fourier coefficients is not independent
issue_body:
I was working on unit tests for the `fft_coefficient` feature calculator, when I stumbled about the following bug:

``` Python

i = np.linspace(-1, 1, num=100)
x = np.sin(i*np.pi)

for max_lag in range(1, 10):
    ...:     print(np.fft.rfft(x, max_lag).real)
# will print
[ -1.22464680e-16]
[-0.06342392  0.06342392]
[-0.19001637  0.09500819]
[-0.37926762  0.12659245  0.12608271]
[-0.6304156   0.15831485  0.15689295]
[-0.94244905  0.19039278  0.18734766  0.18696817]
[-1.31411151  0.22311752  0.21741968  0.21651855]
[-1.74390642  0.25685141  0.24710692  0.24544456  0.24510063]
[-2.23010315  0.29202479  0.27642732  0.27368103  0.27291845]
```

So what is happening here? The value of a fft coefficient depends on the maxlag that is used to calculate the fft.

Why is this bad? 

This means that the value of e.g. the Fourier coefficient for coefficient==3 depends on the configuration for the other features. This breaks our convention that the value of a feature only depends on his configuration

How can we fix this? I see multiple solutions

- make the `fft_coefficient` a "simple" function calculator instead of "combine" (this is computational expensive because it will calculate one fft for EVERY coefficient..)
- add an additional parameter `maxlag` (not user friendly)
- use length of individual time series as maxlag (will Also increase the computational complexity)

I tend to include the maxlag parameter as I do not want to increase the runtime, speed is everything ;)

It would be very helpful to have somebody with a background in signal processing to comment on this. I had a lecture on Fourier transformations but that was 5 years ago, so my knowledge is limited

issue_title: decode_hex fails for Python3
StateDownloader should retry immediately after receiving empty reply from peer
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

During a state sync, sometimes a peer won't have the trie nodes we requested, and so will send us an empty `NodeData` msg.  When that happens we just move on and those nodes are re-requested only after the timeout (20s) has passed.

### How can it be fixed

Whenever we get an empty `NodeData` from a peer, we should look up the nodes that were originally requested to that peer and re-request them to a different peer.

issue_title: Notes on probable bugs in filters
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

There are *probably* bugs lurking in contract event filters that filter on data values.

> None of these have actually been verified, this is strictly from me looking over the code.

#### 1. Variable length data elements.

suppose the log data has an ABI with a variable length value *before* other values. `MyEvent(string,bytes32)`

The code which constructs the regex here: https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/web3/utils/filters.py#L138 assumes fixed length placeholders for all variable length data elements which I don't believe is correct.

#### 2. List types

suppose my event has a list type in it like `MyEvent(bytes2[])`

To filter on this value, one might pass in arguments like `filters={'arg_a': [b'aa', b'xx']}`.

However, when the data filters are constructed: https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/web3/utils/events.py#L99

With *normal* single value arguments, if you want to filter against *multiple* options, you pass those options in as a list.  For single values, they should be passed in *singularly*.  For example, to filter against a single `uint` you would do `filters={'arg_X': 1234}` and to filter against multiple options for field `arg_X`, `filters={'arg_X', [1234, 4321]}`.

In the linked code in the `events.py` utils, it assumes that anything that `is_list_like` has already been wrapped in a list, which will result in an error either during execution, or an incorrectly constructed regex.

#### 3. dynamic sized types.

This one I'm **not very sure of at all**.  however,

The data filter regex is constructed using `encode_single`, and then all of the values are joined together to form the regex.  However, this is not how ABI encoding works... as dynamic sized things are actually shoved to a later offset in the encoded data.  **if** events encode their data field the same way, the regex won't match.


### How can we fix this.

All three of these issues deal with regex issues....

https://blog.codinghorror.com/regular-expressions-now-you-have-two-problems/


So, lets get rid of the regex.  Rather than regexing the data, lets instead just *try* decoding it using the event ABI, and then matching against the provided arguments in their native python form.

issue_title: Generated archive_name in package.json doesn't contain build_tag
issue_body: As per [file naming convention](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0491/#file-name-convention) a build-tag can be specified as an argument to wheel. But currently specifying a tag has no impact on archive_name.

issue_title: conda create does not lock anything
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...**
  - [x] bug report
  - [ ] feature request


## Current Behavior

Conda apparently does not make use of locks when modifying environments or package cache.


### Steps to Reproduce

```
conda create -n env1 --file env.txt &
conda create -n env2 --file env.txt &
conda create -n env3 --file env.txt
```


## Expected Behavior

Parallel invokation of `conda` commands should be handled in some sensible fashion. The simplest may be outright denial to have more than one `conda` instance operating on the same conda installation. Neat would of course be fine-grained locking of indices, package-cache entries and environments with conda instances neatly waiting on one another.

Curiously, there is a module `conda.lock`, but it does not appear to be used by e.g. `conda create`? Was locking perhaps previously in place but lost at some point?

issue_title: Conda create --no-deps not working as expected
issue_body:
Using  `--no-deps` will correctly not install dependencies but inexplicably requires them to be in-place.

Using conda to make a staging area to deploy packages. It should be possible to place all conda packages in a sub directory, index it with `conda index` and proceed with a `conda create`. 
conda is forcing me to place packages that I don’t wish to deploy.

Summary: If you use `--no-deps` it should not require dependencies to be available if its not going to install them.

issue_title: Pinned packages in package structure
issue_body:
Hey guys, 

I have a package A which depends on package B. I build both myself. They both depend on various third-party libraries. 
Now I would like to be able to pin for example `pandas` to version `x`. I get the error: 
`Error Unsatisfiable package specifications` in various cases: 
1) Pin in package A to `x` and no pin in package B
2) Pin in package A to `x` and pin in package B to `x`. 

I could understand that in 1), "no pin in B" can be interpreted as newest version and therefore constitute a conflict. But 2) seems puzzling to me. 

Am I overlooking something here?

issue_title: Using system install of conda
issue_body:
I'm experimenting with using a system install of conda/conda-env in /usr/bin.  The problem I'm running into is:
